coredata.dc:title: Theranostic mesoporous silica nanoparticles made of multi-nuclear gold or carbon quantum dots particles serving as pH responsive drug delivery system
coredata.dc:creator: Akbarian, Mohsen
Gholinejad, Mohammad
Mohammadi-Samani, Soliman
Farjadian, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles are promising candidates to deliver proteins, antibodies, and anticancer drugs and to be served as an adsorbent of various types of substrate. Herein, a theranostic approach, in the preparation of fluorescence-type mesoporous silica for pH-responsive delivery of anticancer drug, doxorubicin, is introduced. By this mean, three types of mesoporous silica were synthesized. The ordinary type of mesoporous silica nanoparticles containing propylamine functional groups was synthesized by a controlled-size technique. Two types of fluorescence mesoporous silica were prepared, doped with gold or quantum dots nanoparticles. The fluorescence imaging has shown that the optical properties of the gold or carbon quantum dots have been transferred to the mesoporous matrix in doping processes. The as-prepared mesoporous materials were conjugated to doxorubicin via Schiff-base linkage. Several characterization techniques were utilized to detect the structure of the particles including FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, N2 adsorption/desorption, transition, and scanning electron microscopy. The amount of conjugated doxorubicin was analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The pH-responsive character was proved assessing the release profile in different pH ranges including physiological pH. The safety of the particles was evaluated by cell cytotoxicity assay on breast cancer cell line, MCF-7. As-prepared particles showed no cytotoxic behavior, but when they were loaded by doxorubicin, the appreciable release of the drug and subsequent cytotoxicity resulted against MCF-7 cells. The hemolytic study was conducted to prove the biocompatibility of these particles in the blood. Altogether, the proposed materials are suitable to be considered as safe and to be used as a theranostic platform for therapeutic and imaging purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Gold nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles
Doxorubicin
Drug delivery
pH responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MSN containing multinuclear GNP (GNP@MSN)
Cell culture and in vitro cytotoxicity assay
Synthesis and characterization of MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN
Studying the bonding durability of the nuclei to mesoporous silica matrix

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude to Research Council of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences to support this work (Grant No. 97-01-106-18479/15873 ) under Dr. Mohsen Akbarian Postdoc project. Also, the authors wish to thank Dr. Laleh Khojasteh at the Research Consultation Center (RCC) of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences for his invaluable assistance with language editing for this manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesized nanoparticles; GNP@MSN@DOX, MSN@DOX and CQD@MSN@DOX. A) In the first steps, the mixture of CTAB in an ammonia solution of water and ethanol are formed micelles while having critical micelle concentrations. In the presence of CQD particles, the possible contribution of CQDs are to be trapped inside micelles. For GNPs with citrate coating, the bilayer micelles could be formed. B) By adding TEOS and APTES, sol-gel processes started. In a condensation reaction between TEOS and APTES hybrid meso-structure are formed around micelles. C) After removing CTAB and purifications, the wall of the pores were decorated by n-isopropyl amine which introduced by APTES. Finally, DOX conjugated to the structure by Schiff-base reaction between amine group and carbonyl group of chain-ketone available in the DOX structure.
FTIR analysis of the synthesized MSN, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN.
XRD patterns of MSN, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the applied nanomaterials.
BET and BJH analyses of the studied nanomaterials.
A) Indicates BET analysis and B) shows BJH pore size distribution plots of MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN.
X‐ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra of CQD@MSN and GNP-MSN a (C 1s) and b (N 1s) stand for CQD@MSN while c (Au 4f) indicate Au regions.
Dispersed and fine ordered pictures of nano-sized spherical MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN samples with smooth surfaces.
TEM study of the synthesized nanoparticles.
TEM imaging of the mixed format of the examined nanoparticles.
DLS patterns of MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN nanoparticles.
Fluorescence study of MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN as a function of time.
The DOX release profiles for A)MSN@DOX, B) GNP@MSN@DOX and C) CQD@MSN@DOX.
Viability of MCF-7 cells in the presence of bared MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN nanoparticles.
MCF-7 cell viability of MSN@DOX, GNP@MSN@DOX, and CQD@MSN@DOX nanoparticles.
Hemolytic activity of MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN
The obtained physical parameters of the studied particles.
Elemental analysis of the particles in this study.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The data were analyzed in the MOXTEK's AP1.3 window.

head.ce:title: Theranostic mesoporous silica nanoparticles made of multi-nuclear gold or carbon quantum dots particles serving as pH responsive drug delivery system

Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences, (IASBS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Basic Sciences &Modern Technologies (RBST), Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences, (IASBS), Zanjan, 45137-66731, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles are promising candidates to deliver proteins, antibodies, and anticancer drugs and to be served as an adsorbent of various types of substrate. Herein, a theranostic approach, in the preparation of fluorescence-type mesoporous silica for pH-responsive delivery of anticancer drug, doxorubicin, is introduced. By this mean, three types of mesoporous silica were synthesized. The ordinary type of mesoporous silica nanoparticles containing propylamine functional groups was synthesized by a controlled-size technique. Two types of fluorescence mesoporous silica were prepared, doped with gold or quantum dots nanoparticles. The fluorescence imaging has shown that the optical properties of the gold or carbon quantum dots have been transferred to the mesoporous matrix in doping processes. The as-prepared mesoporous materials were conjugated to doxorubicin via Schiff-base linkage. Several characterization techniques were utilized to detect the structure of the particles including FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, N2 adsorption/desorption, transition, and scanning electron microscopy. The amount of conjugated doxorubicin was analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The pH-responsive character was proved assessing the release profile in different pH ranges including physiological pH. The safety of the particles was evaluated by cell cytotoxicity assay on breast cancer cell line, MCF-7. As-prepared particles showed no cytotoxic behavior, but when they were loaded by doxorubicin, the appreciable release of the drug and subsequent cytotoxicity resulted against MCF-7 cells. The hemolytic study was conducted to prove the biocompatibility of these particles in the blood. Altogether, the proposed materials are suitable to be considered as safe and to be used as a theranostic platform for therapeutic and imaging purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, design and applications of nanostructures have found their way into many fields of science. In the pharmaceutical sciences, the use of nanomaterials has generally been used to reduce the side effects of drugs, to accurately and immediately deliver therapeutic molecules, to track body-fed or -injected drugs, and even to create a platform for the delivery of two or more drugs, simultaneously [1]. Among all the nanomaterials, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) have gained much hope in the last decade. Due to the specific feature of these materials such as high pore volume and surface area, tunable and uniform pore size, high thermal and chemical stability, simple surface functionalization, nontoxic character, and well-appreciated biocompatibility, these nanoparticles have attracted a lot of attention [2].

The mesoporous silica (MS) materials have been synthesized by various techniques including sol-gel, hydrothermal synthesis, microwave synthesis, modified aerogel methods, template synthesis, fast self-assembly, and soft and hard templating methods. Among them, the sol-gel process which is very efficient and simple being the most common and attractive method [3]. The application of MS materials as drug-carrier was first begun in 2001 [4]. It has been well-reported that drug release and loading capacities of MS could be affected by chemical functionalization of the nanoparticles with proper functional groups [5]. It has also been concluded that amino-functionalized spherical MS has a great drug loading capacity and well-behaved drug release characteristics [2,6]. Nonetheless, these simple modifications are not enough to face complex diseases such as cancer. Some of the other researchers are looking for an efficient system to track an injected- or fed-nanoparticle in the animal body [7]. The system is better to contain a distinguishable characteristic such as a fluorophore or a chromogenic agent that is easily tracked [8]. Challenges in the area of engineered nanoparticles with therapeutic and imaging characteristic have opened a new window of innovation named theranostics [9]. Historically, according to literature survey, the first application of theranostics material dates back to 1940s when both imaging and treatment proposes were joined in the usage of radioactive iodine in thyroid cancers [10,11]. To date, iron oxide nanoparticles (IONP) [12] and fluorophore nanoparticles (i.e. quantum dots) [13] have been used as trackable items in theranostic fields. MS materials are not trackable in their own characteristics [2]; however, in the presence of diagnosable particles in their structure, they could be considered as desirable options for theranostics purposes [14].

With all positive sides mentioned about these classes of fluorophore-MS particles, several gloomy points should be mentioned. It is difficult to fabricate mesoporous structure with homogeneous distribution of other nanoparticles. However, several strategies suggested to construct homogeneous distribution of diagnosable agents inside the pores, like through establishing covalent binding with available functionalities inside the pores [15] or immersion of nanoparticles with MS [16]. However, the embedding of nanoparticles in the pore channels during synthetic processes of MS particles and monitoring the processes is possible through X-ray diffraction analysis and transmission electron microscopy while showing no deformation in the porous structure [17].

Recently, a silver sulfide quantum dots-centered platform with aminated MS shells containing graphene oxide conjugated folic acid was introduced [18]. The aminated MS material, also, well-studied for another application, to reduce protein fibrillation [19]. Similar system was also used to load the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX), and the performance of the drug release was tested in vivo. Based on the obtained results, the system was able to work well to inhibit the growth of cancer cells and also the ability to being imaged due to the fluorescence property [18]. In addition to locating non-tractable drugs, fluorescence labeling of MSN can also help to detect the distribution of drug loaded MSN nanoparticles in the body [20].

In addition to these efforts, many other works have focused on finding nano-structured systems to release drugs in a controlled and intelligent way [21]. The ideal nanoparticles must be able to release their cargo properly in response to site specific stimuli [22]. In this case, there are generally two physical and chemical external stimuli; the first group of stimuli includes light [23], temperature [24], mechanical forces [25], and wavelengths [26], while chemical stimulus includes changes in ionic strength [27] and pH [28]. In general, the research trends of recent years show that the use of pH and temperature stimuli has been gaining more attention, which may be due to the nature of cancerous tissue (high temperature and low pH of tumor site) [29,30]. In continuation of our previous efforts in designing smart drug delivery systems [31–33] and our achievements in the novel applications of MS materials [5,19], in this study, gold nanoparticles or carbon quantum dots nanoparticle embedded MSN (GNP@MSN or CQD@MSN) were used to introduce diagnosable MSN particles for DOX delivery.

Overall, after successfully synthesizing the proposed nanoparticles, we found that when gold or quantum dot nuclei were used, although the pores and surface-to-volume ratio of MSN were reduced, the induced fluorescence property of the particles contributed well, and drug conjugation was not reduced significantly. Also, contribution of the nuclei did not change the cytotoxicity of the prepared nanoparticles, indicating safety of the prepared materials. The aforementioned particles (MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN) were successfully applied in pH responsive delivery of DOX along with conducting in vitro cellular assays on MCF-7 and the hemolytic studies was conducted.

Compared to other suitable nanoparticles as theranostic agents such as IONP, the proposed nanosystems in this study have good dispersion in aqueous media for pharmaceutical applications in addition to their bio-compatibility. Also, other negative properties such as the intrinsic low sensitivity and toxicicity of IONP as MRI contrast probes that have been reviewed in the motherboard of other theranostic systems, do not apply to the system studied here [8].

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and (3-aminopropyl)tiethoxysilane (APTES) were from Merck Chemical Company (Germany). Ammonia solution 32%, hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol, and acetone were obtained from Kimia Mavad (Tehran, Iran). Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was from Daejung Chemical and Metals. Deionized water (DI) was provided from Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C; Millipore SAS, Molsheim, France). DOX hydrochloride was obtained from Thymoorgan Pharmazie GmbH. RPMI (Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium) 1640 was from Biosera (France), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) was from USA Boiler Company. Pen Strep, 5000 unit/mL penicillin, and 5000 μg/mL streptomycin was purchased from Gibco (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sterile dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was obtained from Merck Chemical Company (Germany). For the cytotoxic study, the breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was obtained from Pasteur-Institute (Iran). Glycerol, urea, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), and NaBH4 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). XPS analyses were done with a K-Alpha spectrometer.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out, using a Bruker (VERTEX70, Germany) instrument. Nanoflex particle size analyzer (Malvern Nano, Microtrac USA) was used for dynamic light scattering. X-ray diffraction was recorded by Philips X'Pert X-ray diffractometer, Netherlands. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed by two electron microscopes, Philips CM 10–100 kV, Germany and Zeiss - EM10C-80 KV, Germany. ImageJ software (TEM suite plugin) was used for calculating the size distribution of the nanoparticles. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was done by TESCAN-Vega 3, the Czech Republic. XPS analyses were performed using a K-Alpha spectrometer. The fluorescence study was investigated by a LionHeart, USA fluorescence microscope. Lyophilizing the samples was done by Christ, Alpha, 2–4-ld plus/Germany freeze dryer. A CECIL CE7250 spectrometer was used for recording UV absorbance. The absorption of MTT reagent was recorded by microplate reader, Thermo Scientific, USA.

Controlled-size synthesis of MSN-NH2 was conducted according to our previous report [34]. Briefly, 0.31 mmol CTAB was dissolved in 54 ml of 0.5 M NH4OH aqueous solution. Subsequently, 10 ml ethanolic solution of TEOS (0.35 mmol) and 0.05 mmol APTES were added dropwise to the CTAB solution over 10 min with stirring. The stirring was continued for 2 h and then 0.65 mmol TEOS in ethanol solution as well as 0.125 mmol APTES were added dropwise over 10 min while the solution was being continuously stirred (24 h at 50 °C). For collecting the sample, the mixture was centrifuged (3 × 10 min, 18,000 rpm), and the obtained precipitated residue was washed with DI water/ethanol several times. For drying the residue, the sample was put in an oven (60 °C) overnight. For removing the surfactant (CTAB), the final mixture was stirred in acidic ethanol solution (6 ml HCl 37%/200 ml ethanol) over 48 h at 90 °C. The final product (MSN-NH2) was obtained by three-time washing of the aggregated mixture in ethanol and water, separately. The residual product was lyophilized, and the obtained powder was stored in a safe place.

For the synthesis of spherical gold nanoparticles, co-precipitation of HAuCl4 using monosodium citrate and NaBH4 was considered [35]. To achieve this, after dissolving HAuCl4 in ultra-purified water (10 mM, in 18 mL deionized water), 0.5 mL monosodium citrate (10 mM) was added, and the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min. The reaction was completed by rapidly adding 0.5 mL NaBH4 (10 mM) to the mixture. Produced gold nanoparticles was separated by centrifugation, washed with double distilled water and after drying stored in a dark place.

As previously reported, the carbon quantum dots were synthesized by the carbonization of glycerol and urea [36]. Glycerol (12.5 ml) and urea (1.0 g) were mixed in 10 mL distilled water and stirred at ambient temperature for 10 min. The resulting mixture was autoclave (at 180 °C) for 4 h. After cooling the mixture at room temperature, it was centrifuged (10000 rpm) for 10 min to separate larger particles. The supernatant contains low molecular weight carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MSN containing multinuclear GNP (GNP@MSN)
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize the GNP@MSN, similar to the method used to prepare MSN, nanoparticle was recruited. Along with adding TEOS into the CTAB solution, the synthesized gold nanoparticles were added at a rate of 5.0 mg GNP per 100 mg of MSN. The remaining steps were similar to the synthesis of MSN.

The synthesis of the CQD-MSN was similar to the synthesis of GNP@MSN, except that 7.0:100 mg of CQDs to MSN were added during the addition of the TEOS to CTAB solution. Throughout this process, CQDs were trapped and nucleated into the structure of the MSN.

For fluorescence study of the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN, 2.0 mg/mL of each sample was prepared in the distilled water. Then, after 3 min of sonication in a gentle condition (15 mA), 15 μL of each sample was placed on a glass slide. The slide was kept in a dark place for 20 min until the samples were dry. The images were then taken using the fluorescence microscope. For the control sample, which was MSN, both the GFP and DAPI channels were used. The GFP and DAPI channels were considered for GNP@MSN and CQD-MSN, respectively. This method is also helpful for studying the bonding durability of GNP and CQD into silica surface. For this purpose, the fluorescence intensity of the studied nuclei was measured at three times; 0, 24 and 36 h. To doing so, 2.0 mg/mL (in distilled water) of each nanoparticle was incubated at the intervals. At each interval, the samples was withdrawn and centrifuged (6000 g 15 min) to separate the detached GNP and CQD nucleus from MSN matrix. Then similar to what was done for fluorescence study, the fluorescence characteristic of the residual pellet was studied.

In this study, DOX was conjugated to MSN via pH-sensitive Schiff base reaction. The conjugation is responsible to variation of pH, it would be cleaved at pH 5.0 (near the cancerous cells) while stable at pH 7.4. APTES, introduce amino propyl in the structure of MSN and the Schiff base reaction was performed by interaction between the carbonyl groups in the DOX with the amine groups in MSN [37]. To doing so, a weight ratio of 1.5 MSN, GNP@MSN or QCD@MSN to DOX was selected. Given this ratio, suitable amounts of these nanoparticles (30 mg) and DOX solution (20 mg) were mixed for 48 h at ambient temperature in a dark place. Then, to remove unconjugated DOX, the mixture was centrifuged and washed several times [32]. To examine the amount of conjugated DOX by the nanoparticles, a more accurate method is to reabsorb the drug in an acidic environment. For this purpose, 100 ppm of drug-containing nanoparticles were stored in a shaking acidic environment (pH 2.0) for 24 h at room temperature and then after separating the nanoparticles from the solution and washing the nanoparticles three times (centrifugation 12000g, 15 min), and finally checking the drug level in supernatants, the amount of reabsorbed drug was calculated. DOX conjugated particles MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN are abbreviated as MSN@DOX, GNP@MSN@DOX and CQD@MSN@DOX.

To study drug release, proper solutions containing 100 ppm (in a final volume of 10 ml) of GNP@MSN@DOX, QCD@MSN@DOX, and MSN@DOX were prepared in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) at different pH of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4. After incubation of the prepared solutions at 37 °C, at the defined time intervals, 1.0 mL of the solution was withdrawn, followed by adding the same volume of fresh PBS. To evaluate the amount of the released, DOX, the samples were centrifuged, and their absorbance were read by UV–Vis spectrometer at 540 nm. The final amount of DOX was calculated using a standard DOX plot. By the following equation (1), the cumulative drug release was calculated.
(1)
C o m u l a t i v e
d r u g
r e l e a s e =
C i
× 10 +
∑
n
n − 1
C i
× 1.0
W
Where C is the concentration of the released DOX at the time i, W is the initial DOX mass in the test tube.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: The breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was selected as a cell model. At first, the cell was cultured using RPMI 1640 culture medium (containing 10% (v: v) FBS, 1% Pen/Strep, under 5% CO2, and 90% relative humidity at 37 °C), and then 5 × 104 cells were placed in each well of the 96-flat well plate. After 24 h, the appropriate time for seeding the cells in the bottom of the wells, the old medium was replaced with a new one, and the specified amounts of samples were added to each well. After 24 and 48 h, respectively, a 10 μL/well of MTT reagent (5.0 mg/mL in PBS) was added. After 2.0 h of incubation under the defined condition, all the culture medium was removed, and the 100 μL of DMSO solution was added. The plates were gently shaken in a shaker for 20 min, and then the viability of the cells was calculated by recording an absorption at 570 nm [32]. All the experiments were conducted three times to reduce well-to-well variations. In another line, we studied the fluorescence characteristic of the synthesized nanomaterials in the MCF-7 cells. The cells were incubated by 5 μg/mL of MSN, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN for 24 h in the defined cell-friendly conditions.

The 24 h incubation and a concentration of 5 μg/mL of the nanoparticles were selected to ensure the entry of nanoparticles into the cell and for the least toxic effect, respectively. After that the attached cells were washed with PBS, three times, then proper media was added to the plate. For the well imaging of the nanoparticles-absorbed cells, the experiment was proceed with the GPF filter.

Different concentrations of MSN, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN were used for this assay. Initially, a 5.0 mL of human blood was collected, and a 300 μL of heparin (5000 units/mL) was added. After 5.0 min, centrifuging at 1500 g, supernatant was removed from the cells. The cells, which are mainly red blood cells, were washed three times in PBS and then diluted to 2.0% with the PBS solution. For all MSN-NH2, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN, the mixture of 500 μL diluted blood and 500 μL sample (the dissolved MSN-NH2, GNP@MSN, and QCD@MSN in PBS) were prepared and incubated at 37 °C for 3.0 h. The samples were then centrifuged at 4500 g for 5.0 min, and the absorbance of the obtained supernatant was recorded at 540 nm [33]. To evaluate this assay, water was used as a positive control, and PBS solution was considered as the negative.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, a trend has been opened in the delivery of drug-loaded nanoparticles based on the theranostic use of nanostructured systems. In this study, because of well-confirmed physicochemical properties and well-approved biological applications, mesoporous silica was used. To introduce the fluorescence property to track the theranostic system, gold and quantum dot nanoparticles, which contain fluorescence properties, were used as nuclei in the synthesis of mesoporous silica. On the other hand, DOX drug was conjugated to the system as a model drug using Schiff base reaction. Physical, chemical, and biological studies ultimately confirm that the introduced systems will be good candidates for theranostic use. During the synthesis of the nanostructures, at first, using CTAB surfactant, micelles were formed, and the overall substrate of the MSN was generated by TEOS and APTES. APTES supplies amine groups for the conjugation of DOX. To synthesize the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN nanoparticles, in the initial stages of the overall synthesis and during the formation of micelles, GNP and CQD were added to the solution. The synthetic steps are depicted in Fig. 1 .

To determine the properties of nanoparticles, at first, the FTIR assessment was done to ensure the accuracy of the syntheses. Before entering into this discussion, it is necessary to mention that by combining some materials, their defining FTIR bands are integrated into the spectra of dominant materials, and some characteristic bands cannot be separated. Based on Fig. 2 , peak at 1080 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si–O–Si [19,38,39], and the peak at 1500 cm−1 (NH stretching) confirms that the structural integrities of the nanoparticles are mainly made by silica materials with the presence of an amine group. For the nanoparticle-containing CQD material (CQD@MSN), the material characteristic bands around 1411 cm−1 and 1659 cm−1 were detectable [40]. The first represents the deformation vibration peak of C═C, and the second stands for the stretching vibration peak of CO. Also, there is a peak at 988 cm−1 and another one at 2880 cm−1 which stands for C–N stretching and C–H stretching vibration, respectively. It has been well-known that the CQD particles contain these characteristic groups [41,42].

To further structural analysis, XRD experiment was applied for studying the crystallinity and overall structure of the nanoparticles (Fig. 3 ). As shown, the diffraction patterns of the nanoparticles exhibited some reflection peaks (100 and 110) at low angles (2θ = 2.7° and 4.4°) indicating the presence of ordered channel like mesoporous [43–45]. However, this feature is more vivid in MSN sample in comparison with two other nanoparticles showing the somewhat distortion by incorporating the CQD and GNP into silica matrix.

In order to assess the porosity and other physical factors of the nanomaterials, N2 adsorption isotherms were examined, and the BET (surface area, pore diameter and pore volume) were measured (Table 1 ).

To provide further evidence to prove the mesoporous networks of nanoparticles formed, N2 adsorption–desorption diagrams are presented in the next section (Fig. 4 ). According to the figure, the diagram pattern of all three nanoparticles follows type II, which confirms almost the mesoporous and some microporous structures.

Results indicated with the introduction of GNP and CQD into the MSN structure, the average pore diameter decreased, and consequently their surface area and the volume of each particle reduced, as well (-Table 1).

The BET and BJH diagrams are presented in Fig. 5 . Using physical adsorption of N2 on the nanomaterials's surface, BET technique, the surface areas of MSN, GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN were measured. To calculate the quantity of adsorbed nitrogen on the surfaces, a linear relationship between p/Va(p0-p) and p/p0 was obtained (Fig. 5A). Finaly using the experiemtal intercept and slope from BET, the overall quantities of the N2 in the monolyer of the nanomaterials were measured. By considering the number of absorbed N2 and the molecular cross-section area (77 K, N2 16.2 Å2) the specific surface area of the sample were estimated (Table 1). Also, as can be deduced from the radius distribution of the pores observed by the BJH data Fig. 5B, with the intervention of the gold and quantum dot nuclei in the structure of the mesoporous silica, the uniformity of the pore distribution is slightly disturbed and polymorphism is created.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CQD@MSN in C, N, and Au regions were also studied (Fig. 6 ). XPS results showed three main binding energy peaks for C1s at 284.7, 286.3, and 288.7 related to CC, C–O, and CO forms of carbon, respectively [46]. XPS spectra showed a main peak at 399.7 correspond to N 1s and confirmed the presence of nitrogen in the structure [47]. Also, XPS spectra for the Au 4f region exhibited a doublet peak at 83.5 and 87.1 related to Au (0) species [48].

Besides, the overall morphology of the nanoparticles was also judged by SEM imaging (Fig. 7 ). As shown in this figure, a significant increase in the size of the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN was seen in the SEM images, which reaffirms that the inclusion of GNP and CQD nuclei increases the particle size.
All scale bars were indicated in each images.
For elemental analysis of the applied particles, energy dispersive X-Ray analysis (EDX) was also conducted, and the results were represented in Table 2 .

As seen in Table 2, Si was the dominant elemental content in the MSN nanoparticles, while the carbon was the least. Similarly, the same weight percentage was observed in the GNP@MSN particles with the specified ratio although the Au element was also detected in the particles. However, the elemental contents were significantly altered in the CQD@MSN. According to the table, carbon and oxygen were the major constituents of CQD@MSN particles that could approve the carbonyl groups observed in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1).

Fig. 8 shows TEM images and the size distribution obtained from the MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN. Almost in line with the SEM results, the TEM images also showed that the particle size increased with the inclusion of the pertinent nuclei (GNP and CQD). As represented in Table 1 and by comparing the SEM and TEM images, (cf. Figs. 7 and 8 and Table 1), it can be concluded that with the involvement of GNP and CQD nuclei, in addition to increasing the size of the synthesized particles, the average diameter of the pore, the surface area of the particles, and the total pore volume were decreased. Although the porosity of the structures can be seen in all three images, the images taken from GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN look more compact.
The top panel shows TEM images and the bottom panel displays the particle size distribution obtained by the images.

However, looking at other studies and comparing more or less similar works [40,49–51], it has been found that the gold and quantum dot nuclei in the TEM images appear darker due to the absorption of electrons emitted from the transmission electron microscope. This fact and the results that we extracted from Fig. 8 led us to perform another experiment using TEM imaging (Fig. 9 ). Interestingly, through mixing the control nanoparticle (MSN) with GNP@MSN (Fig. 9A) and/or CQD@MSN (Fig. 9B), in the same image and side by side but with different ratios, the desired contrast was simply apparent. According to Fig. 9A, the control particles (MSN) showed a pale effect in comparison with GNP@MSN particles. Also, the same conclusion can be drawn by comparing the MSN and CQD@MSN specimens. Another point that can be deduced from this experiment (relay on the uniform opacity at the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN nanoparticles) is the monotonous distribution of GNP and CQD nuclei into the two synthesized nanoparticles. In general, it was concluded that the nanoparticles were produced in polynuclear formats.

A shows the TEM image of mixed MSN and GNP@MSN in 2:1 wt ratio while B, in retrospect, indicates the experiment for mixing the MSN and CQD@MSN. Given the ratios that are mixed and the observed abundance of dark and light particles, in panel A, the red circle and arrow indicate GNP@MSN while in panel B the shapes display CQD@MSN nanoparticles. In both A and B, the green circle and arrow show MSN nanoparticles. In this figure, all indicated scale bares are 150 nm.

After obtaining evidence approving the integrity of the structures of investigated nanoparticles, in the next step, the particle size distribution pattern was assessed using dynamic light scattering (Fig. 10 ). In general, with the contribution of GNP and CQD nuclei, the monodispersity of mesoporous particles is slightly disturbed and tends towards polydispersity to higher sizes. This feature was maximized by the involvement of CQD particles in the mesoporous structure. In general, the mean distribution sizes in the case of MSN and GNP@MSN samples were around 80 nm and 110 nm, respectively, while in the case of CQD@MSN, this size was approximately 120 nm (Fig. 10).
Insets stand for GNP and CQD nanoparticles before incorporating them into the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN syntheses.

Various studies have reported the fluorescence properties of gold and carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. In this study, after ensuring the presence of these nuclei in the synthesized nanoparticles, the fluorescence properties of the intervened nuclei were studied using a fluorescence microscope. According to Fig. 11
, it can be seen that the control sample which infers MSN alone did not have a recordable emission by both microscope filters. Nonetheless, the two samples of GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN showed bright points in the GFP and DAPI channels, respectively, indicating emission in these wavelengths (see Fig. 12).

A corresponds to the control sample (MSN), B shows GNP@MSN, and C indicates CQD@MSN. All represented scale bares are 1000 μm. For detecting the bulk aggregates of the nanoparticles, the samples were sonicated gently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the bonding durability of the nuclei to mesoporous silica matrix
ce:section.ce:para: For the therapeutic usage of the synthesized compounds, it is important to check the durability of the connection of fluorescent nuclei to the silica matrix. Sometimes it is necessary for the injected or fed materials to the animal model to be stable for hours in the host body to monitor the materials. Therefore, in this study, we tried to investigate the durability of the nuclei that were bonded to the silica matrix by imaging different hours of the synthesized materials. As can be seen in Fig. 10, no noticeable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the nuclei was observed over time between zero and 36 h, indicating that the nuclei were able to remain in the silica matrix for at least that time.
As indicated, the fluorescence properties of the inserted fluorophore nuclei to the silica matrix were measured between three showed time intervals. All scale bars are 1000 μm.

After determining the chemical and physical properties of the MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN, DOX was selected as a drug model to evaluate the drug conjugation capacity of nanoparticles and its release pattern in different pH media. Previously, our team studied pH responsive delivery and release of different drugs on various carriers [31–33,52]. 21.48%, 18.12%, and 16.87% were calculated as drug conjugation capacity for MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD-MSN, respectively. Retrospectively, the conjugated amounts of 21.33, 17.99 and 16.66 ng of DOX per 100 ng of the MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD-MSN nanoparticles were calculated. The results were the same as the previously published study [53]. After calculating these two parameters, the release profile of DOX in different environments was studied (Fig. 13 ).
A indicates the drug profile for MSN while B and C show the profile for GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN.

Interestingly, although the drug conjugation capacities for the three compounds showed slight differences, the profiles of DOX release for all the MSN@DOX, GNP@MSN@DOX, and CQD@MSN@DOX were approximately similar. However, the drug releases from GNP@MSN@DOX and CQD@MSN@DOX particles were marginally higher than those from the control sample (MSN@DOX). It was also observed in each diagram that drug release occurred in acidic environments at a higher rates and amounts, indicating a pattern of pH-response release for all the particles.
In the next step, the in vitro cell cytotoxicity of the applied materials was evaluated (Fig. 14 ).

In the study of cell toxicity, before measuring the release of the drug from MSN@DOX, GNP@MSN@DOX, and CQD@MSN@DOX and analyzing their effect on the toxicity of the cells, it was necessary to measure the particles’ cell toxicity, themselves. For this purpose, the effect of the particles on cell death was studied at different concentrations (2–100 μg/mL). In general, according to Fig. 14, it was seen that particles can be toxic (near 10% for 24 h and 25% for 48 h) to cells only at very high concentrations, and concentrations below this amount did not have a significant toxicity even at 48 h of incubation. The safety of the synthesized nanoparticles for the studied cells will give more hope for the use of these nanoparticles in the drug delivery.
Nonetheless, via a concentration-dependent manner, MSN@DOX, GNP@MSN@DOX, and CQD@MSN@DOX had a toxic effect for both incubation times (Fig. 15 ).
A, B and C correspond to 24 h incubation of MCF-7 cells with, respectively, MSN, GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN. D and E show the cell toxicity of the nanoparticles in 24 h and 48 h incubations.

Before getting into the cytotoxicity, the absorption of the particles into the studied cell was analyzed (Fig. 15A–C). Given the figure, both GNP@MSN, and CQD@MSN absorbed cells had their fluorescence signals under fluorescence microscope, while because of no detectable such signal from MSN absorbed cells, optical microscopy was conducted for these cells. As to cytotoxicity, compared to the DOX alone, the DOX-loaded nanoparticles had higher toxicity to the cell or bypassing the p-glycoprotein transporters located on apical surface of the cell membrane. Nevertheless, comparing the particles with each other, no significant effect on their toxicity was observed. This observation seems reasonable given the similarity of the pattern and the amount of the released drug in all three compounds (cf. Figs. 13 and 15).

As the last assay on the cytotoxicity of the studied particles, their hemolysis consequence at different concentrations was evaluated (Fig. 16 ). This assay aimed to determine the potential safety of these particles via intravenous administration in animal models. Although the biocompatibility of mesoporous nanoparticles has previously been studied in other studies [54,55], in this study we observed 4.0% toxicity by increasing the particle concentration to the limit of 1000 μg/mL. Nonetheless, this toxicity was generally lower for the CQD@MSN particle than for the two others, indicating a better potential for its use through intravenous administration.
The activity was assayed at different concentrations of the particles using water as a positive control and PBS as a negative control.

The use of pH, as internal stimulants in the tumor site, has many applications in designing a strategy for the targeted release of anticancer drugs [28]. Nanoparticles, on the other hand, allow loading a significant amount of drug to circulate in the body with lower toxic effects [2]. In this study, with the help of amine groups, DOX, anticancer drug was conjugated to the surface of MSN through the Schiff base reaction. Then, using gold and quantum dot fluorescent nanoparticles, two types of theranostic systems were designed to suppress the growth of breast cancer cells, MCF-7. Based on the data achieved in this study, it was concluded that fluorescence particles are evenly distributed in the MSN structure. According to Fig. 1, GNP and CQD could be trapped in CTAB micelles, and for GNP, bilayer micelles are possible due to hydrophobic interaction of CTAB with citrate coating of GNP [56]. However, due to small sizes of nanoparticles (e.g. GNP and CQD) (≥10 nm) their distribution in silica matrices would be possible. Given the detected considerable opacity and the weight ratio of the used nuclei (5:100 for GNP@MSN and 7:100 for CQD@MSN), the observed opacity seems reasonable. It is possible to obtain more separated and distinguished GNP and CQD nuclei in the MSN substrate, using smaller amounts of nuclei [51]. Nonetheless, given the cytotoxicity data, showing that adding these amounts did not cause significant changes of the nanoparticle cytotoxicity, the use of taken quantities does not induce undesirable effects to the whole structure [57]. On the other hand, one must be sure that the number of nuclei in the nanoparticle structure must be enough to produce the detectable signal under a fluorescence microscope. By examining the dimensions of the produced nanoparticles and comparing them with the control sample, it was concluded that the involvement of the nuclei can eventually lead to an increase in the sizes of the GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN and consequently a decrease in the surface ratio as well as their pore volumes. However, due to the observed release profile of DOX, this difference does not seem to have much effect on the drug release. Since the surface area of MSN is very high relative to their mass (area as large as 700 m2/g) [2], the observed reduction in the surface area did not significantly reduce the drug conjugation and even its release. In recent studies, the role of gold and CQD as suitable scaffolds for carrying DOX has been widely discussed [58–60]. Accordingly, the use of the mentioned nuclei by themselves will not have a disruptive role if they do not play an increasing role in the drug loading. Finally, when the toxicity of these nanoparticles was investigated on cancer cells, it was found that despite the addition of two gold or carbon quantum dots nuclei, the toxicity of the particles did not change, and these theranostic nanoparticles could be considered as a safe agent in biological environments. In a recent study, investigating the synthesis of quantum dot and mesoporous nanocomposites, low toxicity and viability of the nanoparticles were reported [40]. The results of this study showed that GNP and CQD nuclei would be a good choice for introducing fluorescence property in other nanoparticles such as MSN. As a suggestion in the future, these cores can be used in other advance polymers such as hydrogels to induce fluorescence characteristic; although for optimal results in other polymers, in addition to in vitro studies, animal modeling analyses should also be considered.

This study aimed to synthesize two types of trackable fluorescent particles that are capable to transport and release drugs. Mesoporous silica substrate was used for particle synthesis according to their previously described properties. In general, it was observed that using GNP and CQD nuclei, the final particle size is larger than the control particle, which was observed more in the case of CQD nucleus. On the other hand, it was seen that the use of these nuclei did not make a difference in the pattern of drug release, and all particles could release the loaded drug (DOX) in response to pH. Alternatively, the presented studies of the physical properties of particles have shown that the use of these nuclei can reduce the surface-to-volume ratio and also affect the size of mesoporous pores, all of which ultimately reduce the drug loading on the particles slightly. Fortunately, studies of electron imaging and fluorescence microscopy have shown that fluorescence property is present in both GNP@MSN and CQD@MSN particles, which can promise the use of these particles in animal models. Considering the rate of model drug release and drug loading values in the studied particles, the values of MTT assays of the DOX loaded-particle did not differ significantly, which indicates the ineffectiveness of the nuclei in this case. It was also found that the studied particles themselves did not have the same toxicity effect on cancer cells as the synthesized mesoporous as controls.

Mohsen Akbarian: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Fatemeh Farjadian: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Validation, Supervision, Visualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to Research Council of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences to support this work (Grant No. 97-01-106-18479/15873) under Dr. Mohsen Akbarian Postdoc project. Also, the authors wish to thank Dr. Laleh Khojasteh at the Research Consultation Center (RCC) of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences for his invaluable assistance with language editing for this manuscript.
